coredata.dc:title: B/N-doping-induced non-radiative relaxation dynamics in graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Peng
Xue, Yuan
coredata.dc:description: Doping graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with heteroatoms is a typical approach to tailoring their electrical structure to different applications. The effect of the doping atoms on the photoluminescence properties of GQDs, especially the non-radiative decay dynamics, is poorly understood. This study investigates the optoelectronic properties of undoped and B/N-doped GQDs using density functional theory (DFT). In addition, the role of B/N-doping in the non-radiative decay dynamics of GQDs has been investigated. Both B- and N-doping break the integrity of the π-conjugated system, leading to midgap states within the bandgaps of GQDs. Electron trapping and hole trapping are two phases of the non-radiative relaxation dynamics in B/N-doped GQDs. For N-doped GQDs, electron trapping is the rate-limiting step, while for B-doped GQDs, hole trapping is the rate-limiting step. These computational results contribute to a better understanding of the role of heteroatom doping in the quantum yield of GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Density functional theory
Fermi's golden rule
Non-radiative decay

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Non-radiative recombination of electrons and holes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Geometries of (a) pristine GQD, (b) B-doped GQD, and (c) N-doped GQD. Pink and blue colors represent boron and nitrogen atoms, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) of (a) pristine GQD, (b) B-doped GQD, and (c) N-doped GQD. More FMOs are provided in Fig. S3.

Projected density of states (PDOS) of (a) pristine GQD, (b) B-doped GQD, and (c) N-doped GQD. B- and N-doping causes additional electronic states to be inserted into the bandgaps of B-doped GQD and N-doped GQD, respectively. The Fermi level is set to 0.
Absorption spectra of (a) pristine GQD, (b) B-doped GQD and (c) N-doped GQD.

Charge transfer lengths and charge difference densities of pristine GQDs and B/N-doped GQDs. The positive hole and negative electron are represented by blue and red, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Variation of absorption spectra with respect to the solvent polarity.
Schematic of different charge recombination pathways of (a) pristine GQD, (b) B-doped GQD, and (c) N-doped GQD.
Electron-vibrational spectra of B-doped GQD and N-doped GQD.
Natural orbital occupation numbers of pristine and B/N-doped GQDs.
The calculated electronic properties of pristine and B/N-doped GQDs.

The calculated non-radiative recombination rate k
nr (×109 s−1), time τ
nr (×10−9 s) and associated properties for pristine GQD and B/N-doped GQDs.

head.ce:title: B/N-doping-induced non-radiative relaxation dynamics in graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, Jiangnan University, No.1800 Lihu Street, Wuxi, Jiangsu Province, P.R. China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology research laboratory, Jiangnan University, No.1800 Lihu street, Wuxi, Jiangsu Province, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Doping graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with heteroatoms is a typical approach to tailoring their electrical structure to different applications. The effect of the doping atoms on the photoluminescence properties of GQDs, especially the non-radiative decay dynamics, is poorly understood. This study investigates the optoelectronic properties of undoped and B/N-doped GQDs using density functional theory (DFT). In addition, the role of B/N-doping in the non-radiative decay dynamics of GQDs has been investigated. Both B- and N-doping break the integrity of the π-conjugated system, leading to midgap states within the bandgaps of GQDs. Electron trapping and hole trapping are two phases of the non-radiative relaxation dynamics in B/N-doped GQDs. For N-doped GQDs, electron trapping is the rate-limiting step, while for B-doped GQDs, hole trapping is the rate-limiting step. These computational results contribute to a better understanding of the role of heteroatom doping in the quantum yield of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a topic of great interest due to their chemical stability [1,2], tunable photoluminescence (PL) [3], and low toxicity [4]. However, their low quantum yields (QYs) drastically limit their applications in bioimaging [5,6], photodetectors [7,8], and photocatalysis [9,10]. Two strategies are commonly used to enhance the QY: chemical functionalization and heteroatom doping. In particular, heteroatom doping is the most accessible method of improving the low QYs of GQDs. Nitrogen (N) and boron (B) are two commonly used dopants for GQDs. They have comparable atomic sizes to carbon and therefore cause minimal stress in the graphene lattice. In addition, B- and N-doping induce various perturbations in the sp2 bonding environment in the graphene lattice, changing the electron–hole recombination in GQDs.

Such characteristics make B- and N-doping of GQDs particularly useful in fluorescence sensing applications. For example, Masteri-Farahani et al. have reported that B-doping enhances the fluorescence of S and N co-doped GQDs [11]. Ge et al. have shown that B-doped GQDs can be used to detect the Fe3+ that leads to fluorescence quenching [12]. Zhu et al. have demonstrated that increasing the concentration of graphite N in the graphene lattice increases the direct bandgaps of the GQDs, resulting in tunable PL QYs due to the negative induction effect of graphite N [13]. Das et al. has reported that N-doping improves the excited-state charge transfer, leading to an increase in the PL QYs of N-doped GQDs [14]. Moreover, doping with boron and nitrogen traps additional holes and electrons in the bandgaps of the GQDs, resulting in enhanced p- and n-conductivities, respectively [16]. This process introduces emitting trap states into the GQDs, providing greater potential flexibility in tuning the fluorescence QYs [17].

Despite the great promise of B-doped and N-doped GQDs as light-emitting materials, the mechanism leading to their high QYs continues to be debated. Experimental reports on the relationship between QYs and B/N-doping are contradictory. For example, N-doping is commonly reported to dramatically increase the intensity of PL [3,10,18], but in a different study, the PL was quenched [19]. In addition, N-doping has been reported to result in a more significant improvement in QYs than B-doping, i.e., 15.8% vs. 1.68% [20]. Both B- and N-doping induce midgap states [21]; however, the timescale of the non-radiative decay depends on the specific positions of these midgap states within the bandgap. For example, Zhou et al. have reported that the energy levels of midgap states can be tuned by oxidation [22], leading to different timescales and mechanisms for charge trapping. Therefore, understanding the precise role of B/N-doping in electron–hole recombination dynamics is key to explaining the differences in their QYs and providing guidance for the rational design of a doping strategy to improve the QYs of GQDs.

Non-radiative electron–hole recombination is considered the main reason for the low QYs of GQDs [23–25], which is the result of the interplay of the quantum dot size [26,27], zigzag sites [5,28], surface functionalization [29,30], and heteroatom doping [23]. In particular, the size and zigzag sites induce the intrinsic emitting state in the QDs, while the edge functionalization and heteroatom doping induce defect emitting states in the QDs [31,32]. These emitting states have different non-radiative decay rates related to the properties of the frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) by the delocalization of the π-cloud in the graphene lattice [33]. Because of the heterogeneity of GQDs and the coexistence of different doping functionalities, it is difficult to experimentally probe the explicit role of doping in the non-radiative decay dynamics of GQDs. To understand the mechanism underlying the QYs of B/N-doped GQDs, a theoretical analysis of the non-radiative decay rate and related factors is required.

Ab initio nonadiabatic molecular dynamics (NAMD) [34,35] and Fermi's golden rule [24,25] are two important methods to account for the electron–phonon coupling effect in non-radiative decay dynamics of nanomaterials. However, a numerical solution to the electronic and nuclear wavefunctions is very demanding in NAMD, which severely limits the general application of this approach to large molecular systems. By introducing the independent trajectory approximation [36], the nuclear dynamics is approximated by Newtonian mechanics, and the nuclear trajectory is propagated along different potential surfaces selected by a stoichiometric procedure. This approach is further developed by introducing the classical path approximation, which assumes that nuclear dynamics can be approximated by a classical path of Newtonian mechanics [37,38]. Through this approximation, the NAMD approach can be generalized to model a molecular system with hundreds of atoms. However, this approach is less accurate for modeling the electron–hole recombination dynamics of molecular systems with large exciton binding energies, which is the case for GQDs. In contrast, Fermi's golden rule approach is based on time-dependent perturbation theory, which resolves the Coulomb interaction between the electrons and the holes. The relevant parameters in the analytical rate equation can be easily obtained from density functional theory (DFT)-based calculations.

The non-radiative relaxation dynamics in doped and undoped GQDs is modeled in this work using Fermi's golden rule. Our results show that B- and N-doping insert the unoccupied and occupied electronic levels, respectively, into the bandgaps of GQDs. These additional electronic levels serve as centers for charge recombination and prevent radiative transfers across the bandgap. Overall, comparing pristine GQDs and B/N-doped GQDs contributes to a better understanding of the role of heteroatom doping in the QYs of doped GQDs.

The initial structures of B- and N-doped GQDs are built by choosing one dopant atom per hexagonal ring. Previous theoretical studies have indicated that when B-doped GQD and N-doped GQD contain more than one dopant atom per ring, the graphene lattice deforms, eventually leading to the deformation dissociation of the structure at high temperatures, which is unstable [52,53]. Previous X-ray spectroscopic studies have also confirmed that no B2C or N2C was found in B- or N-doped GQDs [54,55]. The BC3 and NC3 structures have large binding energies with oxygen atoms that favor oxygen reduction reactions [55,56]. Two pristine GQD models are constructed for comparison, i.e., pristine GQD and pristine GQD*. The former is an undoped GQD of a similar size to the B/N-doped GQDs in this study, while the latter has a similar size to the experimental samples.

The non-radiative recombination rate can be obtained by time-dependent perturbation theory [39]:
(1)
k =
2 π
ℏ
ρ
E
Λ
2
Ψ
N , acceptor
Ψ
N , donor
2
(2)
Λ =
Ψ
e , acc e ptor
H ̂
′
Ψ
e , donor
where Ψ e, acceptor and Ψ e, donor are the diabatic wave functions for the ground and excited states, respectively;
H ̂
′
is the one-electron Kohn–Sham operator; Ψ N, acceptor and Ψ N, donor are the vibronic wave functions for the ground and excited states, respectively; ρ(E) is the density of states of the vibronic energy levels and obeys the Boltzmann distribution, and Λ is the electronic coupling.

Eq. (1) indicates that the non-radiative decay rate depends on the Coulomb interaction between the excited and ground states, i.e., the electronic coupling, and the thermally averaged Franck–Condon integral, i.e., the overlap of the vibrational wave functions. If the high-frequency modes are included (where ℏω > 1000 cm−1), the rate Eq. (1) can be expanded as follows [40]:
(3)
k DA
=
2 π
ℏ
Λ
2
1
4 πλ
k B
T
∑ ν
e
− S
S
ν
ν !
exp
−
ΔE − λ − νhω
2
4 λ
k B
T
where ΔE is the emission energy; λ represents the reorganization energies from the solvent polarization effect (λ
s ) and the intramolecular vibrations (λ
l ) (<1000 cm−1); ω (the effective vibrational modes) and S (the effective Huang–Rhys factor) represent the average vibrational frequency and the average electron–vibrational coupling strength in the high-frequency region (>1000 cm−1); k
B is the Boltzmann constant; ν represents the vibrational energy level and is integral; T is the temperature of the environment; and M is the reduced mass.

The rate expression (3) is a more general form than the Marcus equation [41] since it includes the quantum effects of nuclear vibrations. The calculation details for the relevant parameters in the rate Eq. (3) can be found in Section I of the Supplementary Material.

In general, accurately calculating the electronic structure and optical properties of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) requires a delicate balance between the static and dynamic correlations. Several methods have been developed to calculate the properties of excited states, such as time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) [57], the multiconfiguration method with complete active space SCF (CASSCF) [58], the multireference expansion of configuration interaction (MRCI) [59], and second-order perturbation theory [60].

TDDFT provides reasonable predictions for the low-lying excited states of most molecular systems at a moderate computational cost. In contrast, the wavefunction-based methods are much more expensive than TDDFT but provide more accurate predictions for excited-state properties. The main challenge to calculating the vertical excitations of GQDs is correctly predicting the relative positions of the lowest bright and dark excited states. Shi et al. have compared several high-level ab initio methods, such as scaled opposite-spin algebraic diagrammatic construction to second-order (SOS-ADC (2)), TDDFT with B3LYP and CAM-B3LYP functionals, density functional theory/multireference configuration interaction (DFT/MRCI), spectroscopy-oriented configuration interaction (SORCI), and n-electron valence state perturbation theory (NEVPT2), by calculating the UV–vis absorption spectra of conjugated aromatic molecules using these methods [61]. They found that only the B3LYP and CAM-B3LYP functionals were consistent with DFT/MRCI and NEVPT2 in terms of the order and excitation energies of the three lowest excited states, while the other methods largely deviated from the DFT/MRCI and NEVPT2, especially for large numbers of benzene rings. However, the excitation energy of the first excited state was greatly overestimated by the range-separated hybrid functional. This indicated that the hybrid functional provided a correct prediction of the three lowest excited states of GQDs compared to the multireference methods and that the accuracy of the prediction depended on the type of functional used in the calculation. In particular, when the number of benzene rings exceeds 20, the TDDFT method was the standard procedure for modeling the excited-state properties of GQDs, when the balance between computational cost and computational accuracy was taken into account.

The GQD geometries are optimized using the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE0) functional [42] and the 6-31G* basis set. The excited-state properties are described using this functional and basis set and are consistent with the experimental results [32,44]. The excitation energy is calculated using linear-response time-dependent DFT (LR-TDDFT). The Gaussian absorption spectra are then broadened with a linewidth of 0.08 eV, which is a typical value for conjugated systems [45,46]. The excited-state geometry optimizations are performed based on the analytical TDDFT gradient formalism.

The characters of the molecular orbitals are monitored during the geometry optimization to ensure proper state ordering between the three lowest excited states. Although the charge transfer character of the excited states is not described by most hybrid functionals, the PBE0 functional partially describes it. Moreover, the PBE0 functional typically predicts a larger excitation energy than the B3LYP functional due to containing a higher fraction of the Hartree–Fock term. However, the differences in predicted excitation energies using the two functionals for B/N-doped GQDs decreases by 84% compared to the pristine GQD (Fig. S1). The smaller differences in the excitation energies for the B/N-doped GQDs are due to the electron localization effect caused by heteroatom doping, which reduces the electron–electron correlation.

Based on the generalized Mulliken–Hush method (GMH), the electronic coupling is obtained using the two-state approximation [48,49]:
(4)
V =
Δ E
μ 12
μ 1
−
μ 2
2
+ 4
μ 12 2

Here, μ 1 and μ 2 are the dipole moments of the excited and ground states, respectively, and μ 12 is the transition dipole moment. The GMH method is typically used for calculating the electronic coupling between the ground state and the excited states in a π-conjugated system. For example, Pavlov et al. have used the GMH method to obtain the coefficients of the overlap integral in the electronic coupling to study electron transfer on the edges of GQDs [62]. Cui et al. have used the GMH method to calculate the electronic coupling of non-radiative recombination dynamics in GQDs [24,25,63].

The binding energies of B/N-doped GQDs are defined as:
(5)
∆
E binding
=
n 1
E
B / N
+
n 2
E C
−
E
B / N − GQD
N
where E
B/N is the energy of one boron or nitrogen atom; E
C is the energy of one carbon atom; E
B−GQD/N−GQD is the total energy of the B- or N-doped GQD; and n 1, n 2, and N are the number of dopants and carbon atoms in doped and undoped GQD.

The cohesive energy of pristine GQD is defined as:
(6)
∆
E binding
=
nE C
−
E
pristine
GQD
N
where E
pristine GQD is the total energy of pristine GQD; and n is the number of carbon atoms.

The work function is the minimum thermodynamic energy required to remove an electron from a semiconductor to the vacuum level and can be calculated from:
(7)
Φ =
IE + EA
2

Here, IE and EA represent the ionization and affinity energies, respectively. IE is the energy difference between neutral and positively charged molecules from single-point energy calculations. Similarly, EA is the energy difference between the neutral and negatively charged molecules.

The charge transfer length, ∆Ω, is used by the Multiwfn software [51] to define the extent of the exciton localization. The stronger the localization of the excitons, the higher the ∆Ω value. The formula for ∆Ω is:
(8)
∆ Ω =
∑ ij
Λ ij 2
φ i
r →
φ i
−
φ j
r →
φ j
∑ ij
Λ ij 2
where φ
i and φ
j are the Kohn–Sham orbitals; i and j represent the occupied and unoccupied energy levels, respectively; and
r →
is the transition coefficient of the excited state determined by the TDDFT calculations.

The charge transfer length, ∆Ω, is based on the charge centers of the orbitals involved in the excitations and can be interpreted as the hole–electron distance [64]. It was found that ΔΩ > 2.0 corresponds to a charge transfer excitation in the case of a π-conjugated system [64]. Due to the increased density of the π-electron network in graphene and graphene oxide QDs, the length of the charge transfer is a useful qualitative descriptor of the charge transfer character of a transition [65].

All calculations account for the solvent effect using the conductor-like polarizable continuum model (CPCM) [47]. The geometry optimization and LR-TDDFT calculations are performed using the Gaussian09 software package [43].

Some PAH molecules exhibit radical characters or multireference ground states due to delocalized π-electrons that increase the electron correlation. GQDs are PAHs containing only six-membered rings. The DFT method does not adequately describe the GQDs with significant radical character. To investigate the radical characters of the lowest excited states in doped and undoped GQDs, the natural orbital occupation numbers (NOONs) are calculated using the CASSCF method. The NOONs (see Table 1 ) for the three highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (MOs) are studied. These are the most important orbitals for the three lowest excited states in pristine and B/N-doped GQDs. The three highest occupied MOs and the lowest unoccupied MOs in pristine GQDs are non-radical, suggesting that they are very stable singlet states. The results of this study agree with those of Yeh et al. [66], who found that the arrangement of the aromatic rings determines the radical character of nonlinear PAH molecules. In particular, the hexagonal GQDs are Kekulé structures with stable singlet ground states.

In contrast, the highest occupied and lowest unoccupied MOs, i.e., HOMO and LUMO, show strong radical characters in B/N-doped GQDs, while HOMO-1, HOMO-2, LUMO+1, and LUMO+2 show only partially radical characters in B/N-doped GQDs. This suggests that the low-lying excited states associated with these orbitals have multireference characteristics that cannot be modeled by a single-reference method, such as TDDFT. Therefore, the n-electron valence state perturbation theory (NEVPT2) method implemented in the MOLCAS software package [67] is used to calculate the emission energies for the excited states in B/N-doped GQDs. In addition, the singlet- and triplet-ground-state energies of B/N-doped GQDs (Table 2 ) are also calculated since breaking the Kekulé structure of the PAH molecule could lead to an open shell structure. The DFT and NEVPT2 calculations show that the triplet ground state is the minimum energy configuration for N-doped GQDs, while the singlet ground state is the minimum energy configuration of B-doped GQDs. This is because N-doping increases the electron density and, thus, the electron delocalization of the π-conjugated system, while B-doping decreases the electron density and, thus, the electron delocalization in the π-conjugated system. This leads to a relatively larger radical character in N-doped GQD than in B-doped GQD, resulting in a triplet ground state in N-doped GQD.

Table 2 shows that the electronic properties calculated by the DFT and NEVPT2 methods show similar trends with respect to the doping effect, indicating that the combination of the PBE0 functional and 6-31G* basis set provides a qualitatively correct description of the electronic structures of B/N-doped GQDs. The bandgaps calculated by the NEVPT2 method are smaller than those calculated by the DFT method due to the multireference nature of HOMO and LUMO orbitals. Since the optical properties of GQDs are related to their electronic structures, the TDDFT method is expected to provide a qualitatively correct description of the excited states in doped GQDs. Previous works support this conclusion. For example, Niu et al. have used the TDDFT method to model the absorption and emission spectra of GQDs with different N functionalities. They found that the TDDFT method captured the fundamental properties of the excited states in N-doped GQDs, such as the characters of the MO and the oscillator strengths [33]. Sharma et al. have investigated the energy conversion efficiency in B/P-doped GQDs by calculating the photoexcitation using the TDDFT method [50]. In addition, Bauschlicher et al. have found that the TDDFT method accurately captured the vibrational frequencies of PAH molecules [68]. The TDDFT method has become a common method for studying the optoelectronic properties of doped GQDs. Therefore, the TDDFT method is used to calculate the absorption spectra and optimize the excited states in pristine and B/N-doped GQDs, while their emission energies are calculated using the NEVPT2 method.

Fig. 1 shows the computational models for pristine GQD, the B-doped GQD, and the N-doped GQD. Such models have been used previously to study the electronic structures and optical properties of B- and co-doped GQDs [75,76]. The variation of the doping positions does not qualitatively change the electronic structures of B/N-doped systems. The average CC bond lengths in the three optimized structures are ~1.42 Å, which agrees with previous studies (1.42 Å) [50]. The average CB bond length is longer than the average CC bond length (1.58 Å), while the average CN bond length remains almost the same as the average CC bond length, consistent with previous results [69]. This similarity is because the nitrogen atom has an extra valence electron compared to the carbon atom. The extra electrons of the nitrogen dopants are delocalized in the GQD via the carbon skeleton and, therefore, do not affect the CN bond length. In contrast, the boron atom has one fewer valence electron than the carbon atom. Consequently, the repulsion between the boron atom and the carbon atom increases, and the bond length increases. Such a change in the geometric structures of B- and N-doped GQDs leads to variations in their electronic properties.

The binding energies of pristine GQD and B/N-doped GQDs are calculated to investigate their structural stability (Table 2). The cohesive energy of pristine GQD is 9.017 eV and increase with doping. The difference in cohesive energy between B-doped and N-doped GQDs is small due to the similar atomic radii of nitrogen and boron, consistent with a previous report [50]. Moreover, doping increases the dipole moments of B-doped and N-doped GQD. This effect is more pronounced for B-doped GQD because the larger structural distortion around the boron atom breaks the integrity of the π-conjugated system and induces more local dipoles. In addition, the different electronegativities of the boron and nitrogen atoms cause different charge redistributions, which also contribute to the differences in their dipole moments [70]. To further investigate the electronic properties of the three molecular systems, their work functions are calculated. Doping with boron increases the work function, while doping with nitrogen decreases the work function, consistent with their electron-deficient and electron-rich natures compared to the carbon atom.

Previous theoretical studies [34,50] have suggested that doping alters the bonding environment, leading to variation in the π-electron densities near the Fermi levels. Consequently, doping leads to additional electronic states in the bandgaps of B-doped and N-doped GQDs. These mid-bandgap states serve as centers for trapping charges that block radiative transitions and prevent the GQDs from fluorescing. Moreover, hybridizations in the FMOs of B/N-doped GQDs are uneven at occupied and unoccupied energy levels. In particular, the hybridization in the occupied FMOs of B-doped GQDs is larger than that in the unoccupied FMOs, and the opposite is true for N-doped GQDs.

The order of the bandgaps for the three molecular systems is: pristine GQD (3.08 eV) > N-doped GQD (1.44 eV) > B-doped GQD (0.88 eV). The calculated bandgap of pristine GQD agrees well with previous experimental observations of the PL emission energy (3.06 eV) of the pristine GQD [17]. The bandgap of B-doped GQD is smaller than that of N-doped GQD because the B-doping withdraws electrons from the neighboring carbon atoms, while the N-doping donates electrons to neighboring carbon atoms. In the former case, the electron density in the π-conjugated system decreases, which reduces the screening effect and increases the electron–electron correlation in the B-doped GQD. Accordingly, the bandgap decreases due to the enhanced quasiparticle correlation effect [71]. In contrast, N-doping donates electrons to the neighboring carbon atoms, which increases the screening effect. Accordingly, the electron–electron correlation decreases, resulting in a larger bandgap than B-doped GQD. This interpretation is consistent with the results of Niu et al. [33]: when the hybridization is larger at unoccupied energy levels than at occupied energy levels, a larger bandgap occurs.

Furthermore, the vertical excitation energies of the two lowest excited states of pristine GQD calculated with the hybrid PBE0 and range-separated LC-wPBE functionals are compared, in Table S1. The optical excitation energies calculated with PBE0 agree well with those obtained from the higher-level ab initio method (DFT/MRCI, ΔE
S1 : 1.98 eV; ΔE
S2 : 2.25 eV) [61]. The excitation energies of the S 1 and S 2 states are greatly overestimated by the LC-wPBE functional, demonstrating that the hybrid PBE0 functional more accurately simulates the optoelectronic properties of GQD materials.

Compared to the pristine GQD, the absorption spectra of B- and N-doped GQDs were redshifted (Fig. 4 ), which has also been observed experimentally [17]. In particular, the absorption spectra of B-doped GQD are more redshifted than those of N-doped GQD, consistent with the electronic structures described earlier. In addition, the main absorption peaks in B/N-doped GQDs are blue-shifted (dashed line in Fig. 4). Such a phenomenon has also been observed in carbon nanotubes and is caused by functionalization-induced defect states on their surface [74], similar to the doping-induced surface defects in the carbon skeleton of GQDs.

The intensities of the main absorption peaks were also attenuated by the hybridization of the FMOs [33]. The main absorption peak in pristine GQD consists of degenerate S 3/S 4 states (Supplementary Fig. S2). The B- and N-doping breaks the degeneracies of the S 3/S 4 states, leading to the emergence of multiple absorption peaks in the low-energy region. For pristine GQD, the first two bright transitions (i.e., S 0→3 and S 0→4) contain the contributions of the degenerate HOMO/HOMO-1 and LUMO/LUMO+1 orbitals with π → π ∗ character (Supplementary Fig. S2), which is consistent with previous theoretical studies [33,73]. For B-doped GQD, the electronic transitions from the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) edges to the midgap states correspond to S 1→0 and S 5→4, respectively, while for N-doped GQD, the corresponding transitions are S 4→3 and S 1→0, respectively. The lowest excited states, that is, S 1→0, are optically dark in the three systems.

The nature of the S 1→0 states is further explored by analyzing the charge difference densities (CDD) in pristine GQD and B/N-doped GQDs (Fig. 5 ). The CDD of pristine GQD was a typical π → π ∗ transition, while the B- and N-doping caused electron localization. The charge transfer length, ∆Ω, characterizes the localization of the charge distribution; a larger charge transfer length indicates a more localized charge distribution. In particular, if the charge transfer length of the lowest excited state is >2, it is a charge transfer state with significant separation between photoexcited electrons and holes; otherwise, it is a tightly bound electron–hole pair [65]. The ∆Ω of the S 1 states are: GQD > N-doped GQD > B-doped GQD. In particular, the ∆Ω of the pristine and B/N-doped GQDs were much smaller than 2, indicating that they are not charge transfer states but tightly bound electron–hole pairs. Substituting boron or nitrogen atoms for carbon atoms increases the delocalization of the electron density, as evidenced by their charge transfer lengths.

GQDs are usually synthesized in organic solvents [71,72]; therefore, the dynamic configuration of the solvent near the GQD surface is crucial for understanding the non-radiative charge transfer. Fig. 6 shows the absorption spectra calculated in different solvents. The absorption wavelengths of the main absorption peaks in pristine and B/N-doped GQDs are redshifted, and the intensities are stronger in the solvent than in vacuum. In particular, B-doping causes a redshift of the low absorption peak (denoted by n2) and a blue shift of another low absorption peak (denoted by n4) in the solvent. The intensities of both absorption peaks increased in the solvent compared to vacuum. In contrast, N-doping only increases the intensity of the low absorption peaks without any shift in the peak centers. This indicates that the solvent induces more dipole moments in the low-energy region of the B-doped GQD, leading to a relatively large reorientation or relaxation of the oscillator strengths in B-doped GQD. This feature is due to the relatively large electron correlation effect leading to a larger surface potential in B-doped GQD compared to N-doped GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Non-radiative recombination of electrons and holes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 illustrates the different pathways of charge recombination for the three molecular systems. For B- and N-doped GQDs, charge recombination consists of two subprocesses, electron trapping and hole trapping, which are due to the insertion of midgap states into their bandgaps, as explained in Section 3.1. The low-lying excited states in B/N-doped GQDs are optimized. The MO characters of each optimized excited state are monitored during optimization. Table 2 shows the calculated rate constants and associated properties for B/N-doped GQDs. To test the validity of the computational algorithm, the non-radiative decay rates of the three lowest excited states for the pristine GQDs and GQDs* are also calculated (Tables 3 and S2). In particular, the emission energy of the S 1 state in pristine GQDs is calculated using both the DFT and NEVPT2 methods for comparison (the values are enclosed in parentheses) since the DFT method differs from the MRCI method in terms of the excitation energy of the lowest excited state in pristine GQDs [61].

According to Table S2, the S 3 state corresponds to the first absorption peak (660 nm) of the GQDs in the experiment [72], based on the calculated energy gap. The calculated rate constant for the S 3 state agrees with the experimental decay rate (1.5 ns), indicating that the rate expression (3) can be used to calculate the non-radiative decay rates in GQDs. The calculated rate constants of the S 1 state in a pristine GQD increase with decreasing size (Tables 3 and S1). This trend is expected because the decreased physical dimension increases the Coulomb interactions between electrons and holes [3,15], accelerating radiationless transitions.

In a B-doped GQD, the midgap state is at the unoccupied level; therefore, electron trapping occurs before hole trapping. The timescale for electron trapping is shorter than that for hole trapping, suggesting that the process of hole trapping is the rate-limiting step for B-doped GQD. Similarly, for N-doped GQD, the non-radiative decay occurs through the trapping of holes followed by the trapping of electrons since the midgap state of N-doped GQD is at the occupied level. Because of the different timescales between the two subprocesses, electron trapping is the rate-limiting step for N-doped GQD. The total non-radiative decay rate (i.e., hole trapping and electron trapping) is much higher for B-doped GQD than for N-doped GQD because the B atom withdraws electrons from the carbon skeleton of the GQDs, increasing the electronic correlation. As a result, electron–hole coupling increases, accelerating non-radiative decay. Moreover, the total non-radiative decay rates for N- and B-doped GQD are much higher than the non-radiative decay rate of the S 1 state of a pristine GQD, showing that both B- and N-doping promote non-radiative decay. This is because doping at the basal plane and the edge have different effects: doping at the basal plane decreases the bandgap of the GQD, resulting in non-radiative recombination centers while doping at the edge has little effect on the bandgap [33].

The reorganization energy, emission energy, Huang–Rhys factor, electronic coupling, and vibrational frequency are the factors that affect the predicted non-radiative recombination rate according to rate expression (3). The electronic coupling is proportional to the non-radiative rate according to Eq. (3). The lower emission energy and the larger reorganization energy reduce the energy barrier, accelerating the radiationless electronic transition. Moreover, the non-radiative decay is proportional to the vibrational frequencies. The non-radiative decay of the excited state is proportional to the nonadiabatic vector according to the time-dependent Schrödinger equation [15]:
(9)
χ
φ ex
∂
∂
R →
φ gs

Here, φ
ex and φ
gs are the adiabatic basis of the excited and ground states, respectively; χ is the velocity of the nuclear system; and
R →
is the nuclear displacement coupled to the non-radiative transition. According to Eq. (9), faster movement of the nucleus accelerates the non-radiative relaxation.

In addition, the Huang–Rhys factor characterizes the coupling strength of the electronic transitions with the nuclear vibrational modes; therefore, large values of the Huang–Rhys factor accelerate non-radiative decay. Furthermore, the larger the number of vibrational modes in the higher frequency range, the faster the non-radiative decay. This is because the charge relaxation proceeds faster when more relaxation channels are available for non-radiative decay.

In B-doped GQD, electrons are trapped faster than holes since more high-frequency vibrational modes are associated with electron trapping (Fig. 8 ). The larger electronic coupling and lower number of high-frequency vibrational modes associated with electron trapping in N-doped GQD result in faster trapping of holes than electrons. In addition, the electron trapping and hole trapping phases in N-doped GQD have smaller electronic couplings than in B-doped GQD. In B-doped GQD, electron trapping is associated with a larger number of high-frequency vibrational modes than in N-doped GQD (Fig. 8(a) and (d)), resulting in faster electron trapping. Although hole trapping in B-doped GQD is associated with a smaller number of high-frequency vibrational modes (Fig. 8(b) and (c)), the Huang–Rhys factors are much larger than in N-doped GQD and play a major role in non-radiative decay dynamics, leading to faster hole trapping in B-doped GQD than in N-doped GQD. Moreover, due to the increased dipole moments caused by solvation, the solvent reorganization energies are greater in B-doped GQD than in N-doped GQD. Overall, the combined effects of electronic coupling, the high-frequency vibrational modes, the solvent reorganization energy, and the Huang–Rhys factor cause faster non-radiative electron–hole recombination in B-doped GQD than in N-doped GQD, which is consistent with the higher QYs experimentally observed in N-doped GQD compared to B-doped GQD.

Increasing the doping concentration of B increases the non-radiative decay rate of hole trapping while increasing the doping concentration of N decreases the non-radiative decay rate of electron trapping. This phenomenon is because B has a positive induction effect on the bandgap of the B-doped GQD, while N has a negative induction effect on the bandgap of the N-doped GQD. An increase in the amount of B leads to a decrease in the emission energy when holes are trapped, while an increase in the amount of N leads to an increase in emission energy when electrons are trapped. Since the non-radiative decay rate is inversely proportional to the emission energy, an increase in the number of B and N atoms leads to faster non-radiative electron–hole recombination in B-doped GQDs than in N-doped GQDs.

The electronic and optical properties of pristine, B- and N-doped GQDs were studied by performing DFT and TDDFT calculations. The B/N-doping breaks the Kekulé structure of the PAH molecule, giving the HOMO and LUMO orbitals a radical character. This also leads to the triplet ground state in N-doped GQDs bypassing the singlet ground state and becoming the lowest energy state. Although B- and N-doping cause a strong radical character in the carbon skeleton of the GQDs, DFT can still provide a qualitatively correct description of the electrical structures and optical properties of the doped GQDs.

Compared to undoped GQDs, both B- and N-doping increase the cohesive energy of the doped GQDs. Moreover, the B- and N-doped GQDs have similar binding energies due to their similar radii. Since B-doping causes more local dipoles, B-doped GQDs have a larger dipole moment than N-doped GQDs. By breaking the degeneracy of the HOMO and LUMO orbitals, both B- and N-doping induce midgap states in the bandgaps. Due to the stronger electron correlation in B-doped GQDs, the quasiparticle correction leads to a narrower bandgap in B-doped GQDs than in N-doped GQDs. The beak of the degeneracy of HOMO and LUMO orbitals also contributes to the formation of absorption peaks in the low-energy region. The positions and intensities of these absorption peaks in B-doped GQDs are sensitive to the solvent effect due to the electron correlation in the carbon skeleton, while the positions of these peaks in N-doped GQDs are less affected due to the screening effect of the electrons donated by the nitrogen atoms.

Because of the emergence of the midgap states, the non-radiative decay proceeds via two subprocesses, i.e., electron trapping and hole trapping, rather than via direct band-edge recombination. In the case of B-doped GQDs, the large electronic coupling contributes to faster hole trapping, while in the case of N-doped GQDs, the large reorganization energy and the strength of the electron-vibrational coupling are the main factors for faster hole trapping. Overall, the current study provides a unique insight into the role of heteroatom doping in the photoluminescence quantum yield of GQD materials.
Peng Cui: Data curation, Writing-Original draft preparation, Writing - Reviewing and Editing, Validation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Yuan Xue: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: I. Computational details for relevant parameters in the rate expression and II. Figures and tables.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2022.109160.
